slides.heading: You sent the deck. Now know what happened to it.
slides.sub: A private, branded space for each client, and a clear record of what they did with it.
slides.contact: DeckTrail by OrbitQube  |  open source, self-hosted, AGPL-3.0  |  decktrail.com

slides.heading: What changed
slides.lede: The deck is the product. It always was. What is new is what you can know after you send it.
items: You send a proposal that carries your actual thinking, which is the thing you sell.
Most of the time you hear nothing back, and never learn whether it even landed.
In the AI era, a document you send can resurface anywhere, reworded and unattributed. That is simply how work moves now.
So the useful question is no longer only how to protect it. It is how to know what happened to it.

left.title: The usual question
left.body: How do we stop the file from leaving? Forwarding, screenshots, downloads, re-sharing. It is worth doing, and DeckTrail does the parts that are genuinely real.

right.title: The question we start from
right.body: What actually happened to the work you sent? Who opened it, how far they read, and whose name is on it if it ever travels. That is the part you could never see before.

stats.label: what happened to the work you sent
whose copy it is, if it ever travels
all of it, on your server, no account but yours

cards.title: Gated to one person
cards.body: Every recipient gets their own link behind a passwordless sign-in. No public URL, and no anonymous forwarding. Every open ties to a named person, which is what makes everything after it possible.

cards.title: You see what happened
cards.body: Who opened it, which parts held them, how far they read, and whether they finished. Per person and per deck, on your own dashboard, and exportable whenever you want it.

cards.title: Yours, in the AI era
cards.body: Draft it with AI through your own Claude login. It goes out watermarked to the reader at serve time, and known AI agents are turned away at the door. Nothing ever touches our infrastructure.

slides.notes: The two diamonds are the whole security model. The crawler check runs before the sign-in check, so a scraper is refused and recorded whether or not it has a session. The second asks whether this deck is theirs: signing in proves who you are, it does not entitle you to someone else's deck.

cells.body: Notes, a transcript, a brief. Whatever you already have.
One command, and a link for one named person.
Who opened it, how far they read, who tried to scrape it.
The CLI, through your own Claude login. No API key, no per-token cost.
Pushes the deck to your portal.
Pins a version, so republishing never changes what someone was sent.
Gates, watermarks, and records. Per viewer, at serve time.
Your database. Export it whenever you like.
Signs in once, reads their own copy, carrying their own name.

slides.notes: There is no column for us, and that is the slide. The work runs on your machine and your server, from writing to review, with nothing in between that belongs to anyone else.

slides.heading: What it cannot do
rows: Their name is in the photo.
Paste the text into a model
You know who had it open, and for how long.
Dwell time on the slides they retyped.
The attempt is logged against their name.
The link is theirs. The colleague has to ask you.
403 at the door, and it lands in your log.
slides.footnote: This slide is the reason to trust the others.

slides.heading: Why that slide is in a sales deck
items: Because the first three rows are true, and you already know it.
We would rather show you the floor than let you find it with a client in the room.
If prevention were possible we would build it. It is not, so we build what is: deterrence, attribution, detection.
There is no fourth thing, and we will not pretend there is.

cards.title: Self-hosted
cards.body: It runs on your server. Your clients' decks never touch our infrastructure. We could not read them if we wanted to, which is a property of where it runs rather than a promise we are asking you to believe.

cards.title: Open source, AGPL-3.0
cards.body: Read every line before you trust it with a client relationship. Fork it. Change it. There is no account here and nothing to sign into.

cards.title: Your brand, not ours
cards.body: Point it at your website and it reads your colours and type straight out of your stylesheet. It will not get everything: name a colour "cyan" and no tool can know that is your accent, so you finish the job in the console. The deck a client opens looks like you sent it, because you did.

steps.title: Clone and compose up
steps.body: Postgres and one container. The setup wizard generates its own secrets on first boot.

steps.title: Set your brand
steps.body: A browser form, or point the brand extractor at your own site and let it read your colours and type.

steps.title: Publish a deck
steps.body: Write the content, push it, and DeckTrail mints a per-recipient link and invites them.

steps.title: Watch the trail
steps.body: Opens, dwell per slide, completion, and every attempt to take it with them.

slides.heading: About the little mark in the corner
items: Every deck carries a small "Made with DeckTrail by OrbitQube". The licence does not require it and you can turn it off without asking.
We drafted a licence term to make it mandatory, then read the AGPL properly and found it would not have held. So we dropped it before publishing.
We are asking instead. There is no trial funnel and no hosted tier, so a client noticing that mark is genuinely how the next person finds this.
Same standard as the last slide: better to tell you what we found than ship something that reads enforceable and is not.

slides.heading: Send your thinking. Then actually know what became of it.
slides.sub: Private to one person, yours on your own server, and honest about the rest.
